idea-plugin.name: Provenance Recorder
idea-plugin.description:
Provenance Recorder keeps an honesty log of how your code came together while you work on a course assignment. You submit it alongside your code so your work can be reviewed as a process, not just a final file.

It only runs in assignment folders that have been authorized for recording — a project containing a valid, course-signed .provenance-manifest. In every other project the plugin does nothing: no recording, no indicator, no UI.

What you'll see

  • A “Provenance: recording” indicator in the status bar whenever the plugin is active — the only visible change. No popups, no toolbars, no slowdowns.
  • A hidden .provenance/ folder in the assignment where the log lives. Don't delete, commit, or edit it — the seal command bundles it for you.

Recording is meant to be fully transparent — there are no hidden signals. While you work in an authorized assignment, the plugin writes a timestamped, tamper-evident log of the editing process. Here is everything it captures:

Your files and edits

  • The full text of every edit you make in the assignment files — the exact characters inserted or deleted, and where.
  • The contents of an assignment file when it is opened (for files up to 64 KB; larger files are recorded as a cryptographic hash plus a line count).
  • A cryptographic hash of each file every time you save.

Pastes

  • Every paste into an assignment file: its location, length, a hash, and the pasted text itself (its beginning and end for very large pastes) — what makes it possible to tell which code came from outside your own typing.

Where your attention is

  • Which file is focused and when you switch between files; your cursor position and any text you select.
  • When the IDE window gains or loses focus, and how long you've been idle (sampled on a periodic heartbeat).

Terminal

  • Commands you run in the IDE's integrated terminal, including the command text and its exit code, plus which shell you're using.

Your environment

  • The list of installed plugins (and which are enabled), plus a note whenever a plugin activates.
  • Your IDE version and operating-system platform, and the recorder's own version.
  • A one-way fingerprint of your machine — not your name, email, or IP address, just a value used to notice if one submission was recorded across different computers.

Changes made outside your typing

  • When an assignment file changes by something other than your keystrokes — a formatter, a script, git, or an external editor — the before/after hashes, the size of the change, and (for small files) the new contents.
  • Git operations such as commits and checkouts.

Session and integrity bookkeeping

  • Session boundaries and the links between sessions when you reopen the project, the assignment manifest's signature, and a per-session signing key that makes the log tamper-evident.
  • Internal health signals: clock changes, paste anomalies, breaks in the log's hash chain, and any time the recorder enters a degraded mode or recovers a corrupted log.

The log is stored only on your computer until you upload the sealed .zip yourself; the plugin makes no network requests.

Submitting your work

When you're done, run Tools → Provenance: Prepare Submission Bundle. A sealed .zip is produced next to your assignment — upload it with your code per your assignment's instructions.

action.description: Seal the recorded provenance log and reviewed files into a submission bundle.
